subtopic_detail: Antidepressant choice in pregnancy after MDD relapse
stem: You are the perinatal psychiatry registrar reviewing a 31-year-old G2P1 at 10 weeks gestation with recurrent moderate to severe major depressive disorder. Her current episode began 6 weeks ago. She stopped citalopram 20 mg daily 4 weeks ago when she discovered she was pregnant, after which her mood, sleep and appetite have deteriorated and she now reports passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent. She has a history of postpartum depression after her first child. She is breastfeeding-naive currently. She has no other mental or physical health diagnoses.

data_table.Examination: Anxious mood, congruent affect, slowed speech and thought, no perceptual disturbance, no active suicidal ideation or intent, oriented
data_table.Investigations: FBC, TFTs, UEC normal; routine antenatal bloods unremarkable, beta-hCG appropriate for gestation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence paroxetine 20 mg daily as first-line because paroxetine has the largest sample of pregnancy outcome data of any SSRI and has been used successfully across all trimesters in international guidelines without major safety concerns
options.rationale: Paroxetine has been associated with a small absolute increase in cardiac malformations (RR around 1.5) and is not first-line in Australian perinatal guidelines. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

options.text: Defer all pharmacotherapy until after delivery because antidepressant exposure in the first trimester causes major congenital malformations and the safest approach is psychological therapy alone for the duration of the pregnancy
options.rationale: SSRIs (except paroxetine, with some cardiac concerns) do not significantly increase major malformation risk; deferring treatment of relapsed moderate-severe MDD with SI is unsafe. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

options.text: Restart citalopram at the previous 20 mg daily dose because she had a documented good response to citalopram before pregnancy and any switch of agent at the start of pregnancy risks unnecessary delay in symptom remission with second-line agents
options.rationale: Citalopram is acceptable but sertraline has more pregnancy and breastfeeding data and is generally preferred for new initiation in pregnancy; previous response is a reasonable argument to restart citalopram, but RANZCP and Australian Prescriber lean to sertraline as first-line in perinatal initiation. The more correct answer is sertraline as the safer first-line choice when starting fresh in pregnancy after a gap. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

options.text: Restart antidepressant therapy with sertraline 50 mg daily, refer to a perinatal mental health service and arrange combined obstetric and psychiatric follow up, with brief psychological therapy as a concurrent adjunct over the next 6 weeks
options.rationale: Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to extensive data and the lowest milk-to-plasma ratio of SSRIs; treating moderate-severe MDD with relapse and passive SI in pregnancy is mandated rather than expectant. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

options.text: Commence venlafaxine 75 mg modified-release daily as first-line because SNRIs are more effective than SSRIs for moderate to severe MDD and the cardiovascular and persistent pulmonary hypertension risks in the neonate are reassuringly negligible
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is used in pregnancy when SSRIs fail but is not first-line; SSRI (sertraline) is preferred as first-line. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

explanation.summary: Untreated moderate to severe depression in pregnancy is itself associated with preterm birth, low birthweight and poor mother-infant attachment; treatment is therefore indicated when the depression is more than mild. Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to its low placental transfer, low milk-to-plasma ratio and extensive safety data. Citalopram and escitalopram are acceptable alternatives, especially with documented prior response. Paroxetine is avoided in the first trimester due to a small increase in cardiac malformations. SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) are used when SSRIs fail. Lithium and antipsychotics may be required for bipolar relapse or psychotic depression. Perinatal mental health teams provide stepped care including CBT, IPT and shared decision making.
key_points: Untreated moderate-severe MDD in pregnancy is harmful: treat
Sertraline is the preferred first-line SSRI in pregnancy and breastfeeding
Avoid paroxetine first trimester (small cardiac malformation increase)
SNRIs as second line if SSRI fails
Perinatal mental health team + psychological therapy alongside drug
explanation.pearls: Neonatal poor adaptation syndrome (jitteriness, feeding difficulty, transient respiratory distress) occurs in 10 to 30 percent of SSRI-exposed neonates and is self-limiting over 1 to 2 weeks - not a reason to stop the SSRI in the third trimester.

sources.label: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

stem:
You are the on-call psychiatry registrar called by the obstetric team to review a 29-year-old G1P1 day 8 postpartum who was discharged 5 days ago after an uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery. Her partner reports she has had two days of progressively reduced sleep, perplexity, fluctuating mood, and tonight she has been talking about the baby having a special spiritual purpose she must protect.

She is bewildered and has trouble following conversation. She has no past psychiatric history but her mother had bipolar I disorder. The baby is currently with the grandmother.

data_table.Examination: Perplexed, restless, intermittent thought blocking, mood labile, fleeting persecutory and grandiose delusions about the infant, no objective auditory hallucinations elicited, cognitively impaired on bedside testing, no neurological signs
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, TFTs, B12/folate, calcium, magnesium, BSL all normal; urine drug screen negative; CT head unremarkable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate plan?

options.text: Diagnose postpartum thyroiditis presenting with neuropsychiatric features and commence oral propranolol 40 mg three times daily while awaiting repeat thyroid function tests at 6 weeks postpartum before consideration of psychiatric pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Normal TFTs exclude postpartum thyroiditis as the cause; even if thyroiditis coexisted, antipsychotic management of psychosis is still required. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

options.text: Diagnose severe postnatal depression with psychotic features and prescribe sertraline 50 mg daily with brief community psychological therapy by the perinatal mental health team while continuing her exclusive home care with her infant
options.rationale: Symptoms of perplexity, fluctuating mood and delusions days after delivery are characteristic of postpartum psychosis, not psychotic depression; home management is unsafe due to infanticide and suicide risk. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

options.text: Reassure the family that this presentation is consistent with severe baby blues that affect up to 80 percent of women in the first two weeks postpartum and arrange general practitioner review in 7 days if symptoms persist
options.rationale: Baby blues is mild mood lability without psychotic features or perplexity and resolves by day 10; this is psychosis and demands urgent specialist care. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

options.text: Arrange urgent involuntary admission to a mother-baby unit (or psychiatric ward if MBU unavailable), commence a second-generation antipsychotic such as olanzapine and consider lithium for prophylaxis under specialist psychiatric supervision
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency due to risk of suicide and infanticide; urgent MBU admission, antipsychotic initiation and lithium prophylaxis (especially in those with bipolar diathesis) is the RANZCP standard. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

options.text: Recommend her partner takes parental leave and provides 24-hour supervision at home while she initiates community lithium under shared general practitioner and community psychiatric team care for the coming 6 to 8 weeks of recovery
options.rationale: Home supervision is inadequate for postpartum psychosis given the rapid and unpredictable risk trajectory; inpatient MBU admission is needed. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis affects 1 to 2 per 1000 deliveries and presents in the first 2 weeks postpartum with rapid onset of insomnia, perplexity, fluctuating mood, delusions (often centred on the baby) and confusion. Family or personal history of bipolar disorder increases risk 10 to 25 fold. It is a psychiatric emergency due to elevated risk of suicide and infanticide. Management is urgent admission to a mother-baby unit (or general psychiatric unit if MBU not available), second-generation antipsychotic (olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone) with consideration of lithium for prophylaxis. ECT is highly effective for refractory or catatonic presentations. Pregnancy planning with mood stabiliser prophylaxis from delivery onwards reduces recurrence in subsequent pregnancies.
key_points: Psychiatric emergency due to suicide and infanticide risk
Urgent MBU admission (or general unit if unavailable)
Antipsychotic + consider lithium for prophylaxis
ECT for refractory or catatonic illness
Recurrence risk in next pregnancy 30 to 50 percent without prophylaxis
explanation.pearls: Lithium initiated within the first 48 hours postpartum in women with bipolar history reduces postpartum psychosis recurrence from around 50 percent to under 15 percent and is given despite breastfeeding considerations with neonatal monitoring.

sources.label: RANZCP: Perinatal mental health clinical practice guidelines
COPE Australia: Perinatal mental health guideline

subtopic_detail: Dementia with Lewy bodies antipsychotic sensitivity
stem: You are the consult-liaison registrar reviewing a 76-year-old man with progressive cognitive decline over 18 months, fluctuating attention, recurrent vivid visual hallucinations of cats in his bedroom, and recent-onset rest tremor with bradykinesia. His GP started haloperidol 1.5 mg twice daily five days ago for the hallucinations. Today he is hyperthermic, has marked rigidity in all limbs, is mute and barely responsive. His baseline cognition four months ago showed a MoCA of 19 out of 30 with prominent visuospatial deficits. He has no other psychotropic medications.

data_table.Examination: Severe cogwheel rigidity in all 4 limbs, mute, barely responsive, marked diaphoresis, no clonus, brisk reflexes
data_table.Investigations: CK 4800 U/L, WCC 12.4, UEC creatinine 122 (baseline 88), CT head old age-related changes only, urinalysis negative, blood cultures pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase haloperidol to 5 mg three times daily for refractory psychosis, add a benzodiazepine for the rigidity and transfer to a closed dementia ward for ongoing antipsychotic titration and behavioural containment over the next 7 days
options.rationale: Increasing the antipsychotic worsens the antipsychotic-sensitivity reaction; rigidity is part of NMS-like physiology not just behavioural agitation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies

options.text: Cease haloperidol immediately, treat as neuroleptic malignant syndrome with intravenous fluids and active cooling, transfer to a high-dependency setting, and once stabilised commence rivastigmine for the underlying dementia with Lewy bodies
options.rationale: Severe antipsychotic sensitivity reaction with NMS-like features in a man with parkinsonism, fluctuating cognition and visual hallucinations is classical for DLB; haloperidol must be ceased and cholinesterase inhibitor (rivastigmine) is the disease-targeted treatment per RANZCP. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies

options.text: Switch to risperidone 1 mg twice daily because risperidone is a safer antipsychotic in older patients with parkinsonian features than haloperidol and is recommended first-line for behavioural and psychological symptoms in dementia
options.rationale: All dopamine-receptor-blocking antipsychotics can cause severe sensitivity reactions in DLB; risperidone is not safer here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies

options.text: Add oral carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 three times daily as treatment for newly recognised idiopathic Parkinson disease and continue haloperidol for the psychotic symptoms until levodopa shows benefit in the limbs over the next fortnight
options.rationale: L-dopa would worsen psychosis, and continuing haloperidol perpetuates the antipsychotic-sensitivity NMS-like reaction; cessation of haloperidol is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies

options.text: Continue haloperidol at the current dose but add intravenous dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg six-hourly and oral bromocriptine 5 mg three times daily as specific antidotal therapy for ongoing NMS rather than ceasing the precipitating agent
options.rationale: Cessation of the precipitating antipsychotic is the foundation of NMS treatment; dantrolene and bromocriptine are second-line in severe cases but never used while continuing the antipsychotic. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies

explanation.summary: Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) is characterised by fluctuating cognition, recurrent well-formed visual hallucinations, REM sleep behaviour disorder and spontaneous parkinsonism. Antipsychotic medications (especially first-generation and high-D2-occupancy SGAs like risperidone) can precipitate severe extrapyramidal reactions and NMS-like presentations in 30 to 50 percent of patients with DLB. First-line treatment of cognitive and hallucinatory symptoms is a cholinesterase inhibitor (rivastigmine or donepezil), with quetiapine as the safest antipsychotic if pharmacotherapy of psychosis is essential. Levodopa for motor symptoms is used cautiously because it can exacerbate hallucinations. Multidisciplinary care addresses falls risk, autonomic dysfunction and carer support.
key_points: DLB: fluctuating cognition + visual hallucinations + parkinsonism + REM sleep behaviour disorder
Severe antipsychotic sensitivity in 30-50% (NMS-like reactions)
First-line for cognitive/psychotic features: cholinesterase inhibitor (rivastigmine)
If antipsychotic essential: low-dose quetiapine
L-dopa cautiously for motor symptoms
explanation.pearls: DaT-SPECT (dopamine transporter scan) showing reduced striatal uptake supports DLB and PD over Alzheimer disease; it is the imaging biomarker used to distinguish DLB from AD with parkinsonian features.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Dementia with Lewy bodies
Dementia Australia: Dementia with Lewy bodies

subtopic_detail: AECOPD ventilatory failure and BiPAP threshold
stem: You are the medical registrar in the emergency department reviewing a 72-year-old former smoker (75 pack-years) with severe COPD (FEV1 35 percent predicted, GOLD D) presenting with 3 days of increased dyspnoea, purulent sputum and reduced exercise tolerance. He uses tiotropium and salbutamol. On arrival he is using accessory muscles and is drowsy but rousable. He has had nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium plus 40 mg oral prednisolone in the department. Current oxygen is 4 L/min via nasal prongs.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6, HR 112, BP 142/82, RR 32, SpO2 92% on 4 L/min nasal prongs
data_table.Examination: Pursed-lip breathing, audible polyphonic wheeze and prolonged expiration bilaterally, drowsy but rousable, no asterixis yet, no focal chest signs of consolidation
data_table.Investigations: ABG (on 4 L/min): pH 7.26, pCO2 78 mmHg, pO2 62 mmHg, HCO3 30 mmol/L, base excess +4; FBC WCC 12.4, CRP 84, U+E normal, chest X-ray hyperinflation no infiltrate

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed directly to endotracheal intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation without trial of non-invasive ventilation because NIV is contraindicated when the patient has a depressed level of consciousness and a pCO2 over 70 mmHg
options.rationale: NIV is first-line for AECOPD with hypercapnic acidosis even with mild drowsiness due to hypercapnia (it often improves with NIV); intubation is reserved for failure of NIV or absolute contraindication. (Source: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD)
source_refs: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD

options.text: Increase the oxygen to 8 L/min via non-rebreather mask to achieve saturations above 95 percent and continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol plus ipratropium without commencing non-invasive ventilation at this stage
options.rationale: High-flow O2 in COPD type 2 failure worsens hypercapnia and acidosis; controlled O2 (target 88-92%) is the rule, and BiPAP is required for the respiratory acidosis. (Source: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD)
source_refs: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD

options.text: Reduce oxygen to titrate SpO2 88 to 92 percent via Venturi mask and commence bilevel non-invasive ventilation (IPAP 14 cmH2O EPAP 5 cmH2O) for hypercapnic respiratory failure, with reassessment of ABG in 1 hour
options.rationale: Type 2 respiratory failure with respiratory acidosis (pH under 7.35, pCO2 over 45) despite controlled oxygen and bronchodilator therapy is the trigger for BiPAP in AECOPD per Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory and TSANZ. (Source: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD)
source_refs: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes and intravenous aminophylline 5 mg/kg loading followed by an infusion of 0.5 mg/kg/hr without commencing NIV at this stage and reassess clinical response in 1 hour
options.rationale: Aminophylline is no longer routinely recommended in AECOPD and magnesium has limited evidence; neither replaces NIV for type 2 respiratory failure with acidosis. (Source: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD)
source_refs: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD

options.text: Commence oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily and continued nebulised bronchodilators on the ward, deferring any ventilatory support because the chest X-ray shows no consolidation to indicate severe infective exacerbation
options.rationale: Antibiotics and bronchodilators are needed but do not address the type 2 respiratory failure with acidosis, which requires NIV. (Source: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD)
source_refs: TSANZ: Non-invasive ventilation in COPD

explanation.summary: Acute exacerbation of COPD with respiratory acidosis (pH under 7.35, pCO2 over 45 mmHg) despite controlled oxygen and maximal medical therapy is the indication for non-invasive ventilation (bilevel positive airway pressure). Target oxygen saturation 88 to 92 percent using a Venturi mask. Bronchodilators (salbutamol plus ipratropium nebulised), systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 30 to 40 mg daily for 5 days), and antibiotics for infective exacerbations (amoxicillin or doxycycline first-line per Australian guidelines) are concurrent. Reassess ABG at 1 hour - failure of NIV (worsening acidosis, persistent pH under 7.25 at 2 hours, hypoxia, agitation) triggers escalation to intubation.
key_points: AECOPD with pH under 7.35 and pCO2 over 45 = NIV
Controlled O2 88-92% via Venturi mask
Salbutamol + ipratropium nebulised, plus oral prednisolone 5 days
Antibiotics if infective features (amoxicillin or doxycycline)
Reassess ABG at 1 hour, escalate if no improvement at 2 hours
explanation.pearls: Oxygen-induced hypercapnia in chronic CO2 retention is multifactorial: reversal of hypoxic vasoconstriction worsening V/Q mismatch is the main mechanism (not solely loss of hypoxic drive).

subtopic_detail: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema first-line management
stem:
You are the medical registrar in the resuscitation bay reviewing a 68-year-old man with known HFrEF (LVEF 28 percent), previous anterior MI 4 years ago, and hypertension. He has had 6 hours of progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea and pink frothy sputum. He is sitting upright, distressed and unable to complete a sentence. His usual medications are perindopril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, frusemide 40 mg twice daily and atorvastatin 40 mg.

He has not been adherent for the last 3 days. He has no chest pain. The 12-lead ECG shows sinus tachycardia and known left bundle branch block without acute ischaemic changes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 184/112, HR 124 sinus, RR 36, SpO2 87% air rising to 92% on 15 L NRB, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Tripod posture, accessory muscle use, fine bibasal crackles to mid-zones, raised JVP, pitting peripheral oedema to knees, peripheral perfusion warm
data_table.Investigations: Chest X-ray: cardiomegaly with bilateral perihilar opacities and Kerley B lines; troponin pending; BNP 1842 ng/L; UEC creatinine 110 (baseline 102), K 4.0; ABG pH 7.32 pCO2 48 pO2 60 on NRB

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline 50 mcg as a slow push, intubate immediately and commence noradrenaline infusion at 0.05 mcg/kg/min as first-line therapy for cardiogenic shock pending arrival of the cardiology consult team
options.rationale: He is hypertensive and warm-perfused so not in cardiogenic shock; vasopressors and adrenaline are contraindicated in this hypertensive APO subtype. (Source: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018

options.text: Commence high-dose intravenous beta blocker with metoprolol 5 mg slow push every 5 minutes and continue current oral diuretic at home dose, deferring nitrate or non-invasive ventilation until heart rate has been controlled below 100
options.rationale: Acute IV beta blocker worsens cardiogenic shock and pulmonary oedema; chronic beta blocker may be continued but not up-titrated in acute decompensation. (Source: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018

options.text: Administer intravenous morphine 5 mg, an intravenous bolus of 500 mL of 0.9% sodium chloride to support cardiac output, and continue home oral diuretic dose pending observation over the next 4 hours of inpatient management
options.rationale: IV fluids worsen pulmonary oedema; morphine increases mortality in APO per current evidence and is no longer routinely recommended. (Source: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018

options.text: Sit upright, commence non-invasive bilevel positive airway pressure ventilation, give sublingual glyceryl trinitrate 600 mcg and start an intravenous nitrate infusion titrated to BP, and administer intravenous frusemide 80 mg as initial diuretic dose
options.rationale: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema with hypertension and SpO2 under 90 on air is managed with NIV, IV nitrate for preload and afterload reduction and IV loop diuretic per CSANZ heart failure guideline and Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular. (Source: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018

options.text: Cease all chronic heart failure medications including perindopril and bisoprolol now to avoid contributing to acute decompensation, and start oral metolazone 5 mg in combination with continuing oral frusemide as first-line acute therapy
options.rationale: Chronic ACE inhibitor and beta blocker are continued unless hypotensive or in shock; oral diuretic alone in acute APO is inadequate and IV route is required. (Source: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018

explanation.summary: Acute decompensated heart failure with pulmonary oedema is classified by perfusion and congestion. Hypertensive wet-and-warm (this patient) is treated with sitting upright, NIV (CPAP or BiPAP), IV nitrate infusion titrated to BP (starting 5 to 10 mcg/min), and IV loop diuretic (frusemide 1 to 2 times the home oral daily dose IV). Underlying triggers (ischaemia, AF, infection, non-adherence, NSAIDs, salt load) are sought and managed. Chronic disease-modifying therapy (ACE inhibitor or ARB or sacubitril-valsartan, beta blocker, MRA, SGLT2 inhibitor) is continued unless contraindicated by hypotension or shock. Cardiogenic shock (wet-and-cold) needs inotrope or mechanical support.
key_points: Wet-and-warm hypertensive APO: NIV + IV nitrate + IV frusemide
Wet-and-cold cardiogenic shock: inotropes (dobutamine), possible mechanical support
Avoid IV fluids unless RV-dominant or normotensive dry-and-cold
Continue chronic GDMT unless hypotensive
Find and treat the trigger
explanation.pearls: IV nitrate is contraindicated if SBP under 100 mmHg, severe aortic stenosis with low gradient, or recent PDE5 inhibitor use - these are the three traps to avoid before reaching for the GTN infusion.

sources.label: National Heart Foundation/CSANZ: Heart failure guideline 2018
Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure
